title: What is a Cold Room & How Does It Work?
keyword: cold room how it works India
summary: Understand the fundamental thermodynamics and mechanical cycle of walk-in refrigeration units in India.
intro: Cold rooms are walk-in insulated chambers designed to maintain specific temperature thresholds, extending the storage shelf-life of perishables. In India, tropical ambient conditions require robust thermal design parameters.
section1Body: A cold room operates by extracting heat from inside the sealed enclosure and venting it outdoors. This loop relies on four major components: the compressor, the condenser, the expansion valve, and the evaporator coil. Refrigerant gas flows continuously through these components, changing states to absorb and dissipate heat.
section2Body: Without proper insulation, ambient tropical heat will leak through the walls, forcing compressors to run continuously. We utilize Cam-locked Polyurethane Foam (PUF) panels with high-density cores (40 kg/m³) to guarantee a complete vapor barrier, reducing monthly electricity consumption by up to 25%.

title: Cold Room Capacity Guide: How to Calculate Storage Needs
summary: Learn formulas to translate metric tons (MT) or pallet count into physical internal room sizing dimensions.
intro: Sizing a cold storage room requires translating agricultural tonnage (MT) or logistics pallets into physical internal dimensions (Length x Width x Height in feet). Under-sizing leads to poor air circulation, while over-sizing wastes energy.
section1Body: A general engineering rule of thumb for agricultural products is to allocate approximately 5 to 6 cubic meters of space per metric ton (MT) of inventory. This accommodates necessary spacing for pallets, ventilation corridors, and evaporator air blast ranges. For example, a 10 MT room needs roughly 50-60 cubic meters of interior space.
section2Body: Never stack items directly against walls or block the evaporator fan flow. Leave at least a 6-inch gap along the perimeter panels and 12 inches of clearance below the ceiling coolers to ensure uniform temperature distribution.

title: PUF Panel Thickness Guide for Temperature Requirements
summary: Explore thermal conductivity variables of 60mm, 80mm, 100mm, and 120mm cam-locked panels.
intro: Selecting the correct thickness of Polyurethane Foam (PUF) panels is critical to balance structural thermal resistance with equipment capital costs.
section1Body: For standard chilling rooms (0°C to +8°C) such as fruit ripening setups, 60mm or 80mm panels are suitable. Freezer rooms (-15°C to -10°C) require 100mm thickness. Deep freeze chambers (-18°C to -25°C) like ice cream vaults must use 120mm panels to prevent thermal leakage.
section2Body: Vapor seals are critical. Pre-fabricated cam-locks lock panels together, compressing tongue-and-groove joints to create airtight seams that prevent moisture ingress and internal frosting.

title: Government Subsidy for Cold Storage in India 2025
summary: Step-by-step documentation walkthrough to apply for 35% to 50% capital grants under NHB and MIDH.
intro: To encourage cold chain setups, the Indian Government provides back-ended capital subsidies under the Horticulture Mission (MIDH) and National Horticulture Mission (NHB).
section1Body: Standard eligible projects receive a 35% capital subsidy. For hilly terrains, tribal areas, and registered Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs), the subsidy limit increases to 50% of the project's baseline cost.
section2Body: Submissions require a Detailed Project Report (DPR), CAD engineering drawings, panel test certificates, and bank loan approval. Sizing details must strictly match MIDH operational guidelines to qualify.

title: Blast Chiller vs. Cold Room: Which Do You Need?
summary: Differentiate between shock-freezing cooked items in 90 minutes and long-term bulk freezing chambers.
intro: Understanding whether your business requires a high-velocity blast chiller/freezer or a standard holding cold storage room is key to proper capital allocation.
section1Body: Blast chillers are engineered to rapidly pull down temperatures of hot or freshly harvested products (e.g. from +70°C down to -18°C) within a couple of hours. This rapid freezing prevents large ice crystals from forming, preserving cell integrity, taste, and texture.
section2Body: A standard cold storage room is designed to hold already-chilled or frozen products at their target temperature. It does not have the refrigeration power or high-velocity airflow required to freeze hot products quickly without risking temperature spikes and spoilage of other stored items.

title: How to Maintain Your Cold Room: AMC Checklist
summary: Preventative checkups for compressor load current, fin spacing, evaporator fan coils, and door heaters.
intro: Regular preventative maintenance is essential to avoid costly product spoilage and keep your refrigeration systems running at peak efficiency.
section1Body: Operators should monitor temperatures twice daily, check for ice buildup on evaporator coils, inspect door gaskets for proper sealing, and ensure safety release buttons are working correctly. Any moisture ingress must be addressed immediately to prevent ice formation inside the panels.
section2Body: An AMC ensures certified engineers check compressor oil levels, refrigerant charge pressure, electrical contactors, and telemetry calibration. Preventive maintenance can extend the lifespan of your condensing unit by 5+ years.

title: Fruits Ripening Chamber: Complete Technical Sizing
summary: Designing automated ethylene gas chambers and forced-air flow designs for banana and mango ripening.
intro: Fruit ripening chambers utilize specialized controlled atmospheres to ripen fruits like bananas, mangoes, and papayas uniformly and naturally.
section1Body: Automated gas generators release controlled amounts of ethylene gas (typically 100-150 ppm) over a 24-hour cycle. Precise ventilation and carbon dioxide exhaust systems are required to keep CO2 levels below 1% to avoid uneven coloring or fruit rot.
section2Body: Unlike standard cold rooms, ripening chambers require high-volume forced-air cooling. Air must be drawn through stacked fruit crates using specialized tarps or plenum chambers to ensure all boxes experience identical temperature, humidity, and gas exposure.

title: Cold Room for Dairy: Temperature Requirements Explained
summary: Thermal thresholds for raw milk chilling depots and processed butter deep-freezing parameters.
intro: Dairy products require strict cold chain adherence. Even small temperature fluctuations can result in bacterial growth and spoil raw milk or processed derivatives.
section1Body: Raw milk received from farms must be chilled to 4°C within two hours of milking. Cold storage rooms for pasteurized milk, curd, and fresh paneer must be maintained at a constant 2°C to 4°C. Butter and ice cream require deep-freeze holding rooms at -18°C to -25°C.
section2Body: Dairy cold storage rooms must use SS-304 stainless steel interior panels or food-safe pre-painted GI sheets. All joints must be sealed with anti-fungal silicon, and floors must be sloped with drains for daily cleaning washdowns.

title: Refrigeration Compressor Types: Semi-hermetic vs Scroll
summary: Detailed efficiency comparisons of Copeland scroll and Bitzer semi-hermetic plants under high ambient temps.
intro: The compressor is the heart of your refrigeration system. Selecting the right type depends on your cooling load capacity, budget, and operating environment.
section1Body: Scroll compressors use two interleaving scrolls to compress gas. They are highly efficient for low-to-medium cooling capacities (up to 15 HP), run quietly, and have fewer moving parts, making them extremely reliable and virtually maintenance-free.
section2Body: Semi-hermetic reciprocating compressors use pistons. They are the industry standard for medium-to-large industrial refrigeration plants (above 15 HP). Their major advantage is repairability; since the motor and cylinders are bolted together in one housing, they can be opened and serviced on-site.

title: Cold Room Installation Process: Step-by-Step
summary: Witness how our engineers assemble cam-locks, run vacuum piping checks, and run pull-down test calibrations.
intro: A professional installation is just as important as high-quality equipment. Incorrectly installed panels or piping will cause thermal leaks, ice buildup, and premature compressor failure.
section1Body: We start with a level concrete foundation. Pre-fabricated PUF panels are aligned and locked together using heavy-duty eccentric cam-locks. Silicon sealant is applied between all tongue-and-groove joints to ensure an airtight vapor barrier.
section2Body: Refrigeration copper pipes are brazed under nitrogen purge to prevent internal oxidation. The system is pressure-tested with dry nitrogen, evacuated below 500 microns using a vacuum pump, and charged with refrigerant before running a 24-hour temperature pull-down calibration test.
